body_txt: So it's The End of the World as We Know It , or a reasonable facsimile. Chaos, anarchy, and destruction reign. But just when you think it can't get any worse, these guys show up. The Anthropomorphic Personifications of the worst things imaginable, if the Four Horsemen show up, you know it's an Apocalypse with a capital A. The Bible has a job description note only the fourth name was actually said aloud, and the other three are inferred and item for each horseman, and a color for each horse (seen here ).  Some Bible historians have tried to identify them as some historical figures of the time the Revelation was written — historical figures perhaps falsely accused, libeled — but let's not get into that, okay? Most adaptations will replace Conquest with Pestilence , note This is Newer Than They Think , tracing back to the beginning of the 20th Century or a similar apocalyptic agent like Pollution (as famously done in Good Omens ), Genocide , Nuclear Holocaust or Overpopulation . This also helps to more easily differentiate the horsemen, as "conquest" and "war" can seem redundant. note The most important difference would be that Conquest is most often interpreted as a figure representing fake peace, as an Antichrist of sorts, as it bears superficial resemblence to the real King Jesus a few chapters after, but is different at certain points, notably it being given a bow. The only one of these actually named in the Bible is Death; the others are only identified by the horses they ride and the tasks and powers they are assigned. The exact nature, role and purpose of the Horsemen has been the subject of centuries of theological debate — most of all Conquest. The most common interpretation of the first rider is as a figure of incalculable evil, perhaps The Antichrist or Satan , setting out to conquer the world; others interpret him as a benevolent being like Jesus or the Holy Spirit, and his "Conquest" or "Victory" could be the beginning of the final triumph of Good over Evil. The other three horsemen don't trigger quite as much discussion, but they aren't universally agreed upon either. There is even the theory that there are actually five horsemen, interpreting the line "and Hell followed after him" as referring to another entity. Compare Destroyer Deity . For Death when he's a solo act, see The Grim Reaper (or Don't Fear the Reaper for the more benign version). See also Four Is Death .
